evidence_quotes.quote: games are tied to non essential online services that "brick" the game once official support has ended.

paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act seems like a great initiative that aims to tackle a lot of bad online consumer practices that pray on the individual. I am in full support of it's main points and in the spirit of digital rights and protections I want to suggest adding games software as a part of it. Currently more and more games are tied to non essential online services that "brick" the game once official support has ended. Basically you pay for a product that becomes completely unusable once a company decides to no longer maintain it or when it becomes profitable to remove it. Due to that fan-lead preservation(unreliable) and online piracy(illegal) become the only viable ways of accessing older software and games. I think requiring publishers to have an "end of life" plan that leaves the software in an accessible state and a clear way of showing what you actually own once you buy a product is a good way of improving both the product and the overall market. There is already such an initiative called "Stop Destroying Videogames" that currently has over a million signatures, which shows there is a consumer want for such regulations. I implore you to consider games software preservation as a part of the Digital Fairness Act.
